text: When designing REST APIs, it's important to follow best practices to ensure they are easy to use and maintain. First, endpoints should be simple and descriptive, such as `/users` or `/orders`, rather than confusing names. Second, use appropriate HTTP status codes to communicate results clearly, for example, 200 for success, 404 for not found, and 500 for server errors. Third, always document your API well, explaining endpoints, parameters, and examples so developers can integrate easily. Finally, implement versioning, like `/v1/users`, to avoid breaking changes when you update the API. These practices help create reliable, user-friendly APIs.
